documents.title: Southern Methodist University public AI posture sources

ai_strategy.summary: SMU links formal generative-AI guidance with NVIDIA DGX SuperPOD/ManeFrame III research computing, O'Donnell data science support, professional AI training, and an AECOM AI-infrastructure fellowship.

key_provisions: Use institutionally reviewed AI tools for sensitive university work.
Protect restricted, confidential, student, personnel, and research data when using AI tools.
Verify AI output and keep humans accountable for academic, administrative, and research decisions.
ai_policy.summary: Southern Methodist University's public AI policy posture is comprehensive and emphasizes responsible use, privacy, and human accountability based on reviewed sources.

governance_structure.summary: Governance appears distributed across AI-facing offices, centers, technology units, teaching units, or committees; no standalone chief AI officer was confirmed unless named in leadership.

regulatory_context.summary: State/system context was checked for TX. Relevant public AI or system governance links are included where found.

narrative_summary: Southern Methodist University is an R1 institution whose public AI posture is best understood as follows: SMU links formal generative-AI guidance with NVIDIA DGX SuperPOD/ManeFrame III research computing, O'Donnell data science support, professional AI training, and an AECOM AI-infrastructure fellowship. The evidence touches governance and policy, teaching and learning, research and innovation, enterprise enablement, student experience, workforce development, and equity or public-impact dimensions. The strongest public sources are first-party AI hubs, guidance pages, research center pages, academic-program materials, and state or system policy resources. Compared with the most centralized AI adopters, several details remain opaque, including named executive AI ownership, exact enterprise user counts, and total AI spending. Even so, the reviewed public record supports a medium-confidence assessment that AI is moving into curriculum, research infrastructure, technology governance, and student or workforce-facing programs rather than remaining purely ad hoc.

gaps_acknowledged: No public chief AI officer was confirmed in reviewed sources.
No total institution-wide AI investment amount was found.
Exact enterprise AI user counts or contract values were usually not public.
Follow-up search reinforced Microsoft Copilot and syllabus-level AI guidance but did not disclose SMU Copilot user counts, ChatGPT/GPT tenant user counts, license cost, or DGX/ManeFrame GPU count.
